speaker: interviewer
text: And we were talking about the torturous road to Carpathia.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tortuous-- torturous road back to Carpathia-- now, we are-- we are in-- in-- Rosh Hashanah in the city of Horodenka. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we were at the place of Berl Gefner. Horodenka, the city, had already, at that time, a Judenrat and a-- a very, very large Jewish community that was supporting the Hungarian refugees. One day after Rosh Hashanah, my brother Tsvi and I went over to the-- to the-- Judenrat. And we told them that we have a letter from my Ukrainian priest to-- to help us out in our-- in our journey-- return back to the Carpathia. And if it is possible, they could give us some paper to-- also that we should have some kind of paper to cover that we were returning to Carpathia. And for our greatest surprise, the-- the office of the Judenrat in Horodenka told us that we should come back tomorrow, and they will have a permission paper issued by the German command. Up to this day-- the next day-- we-- my brother Tsvi and I-- we always were the ones that-- my brother Moshe was with the children. So he had stayed behind. We were the ones that were going out. Up to this day, I am-- I am wondering if we came to the Judenrat, and here they gave us a document that had the stamp with the swastika on it, signed by a German Oberleutant, and our names, and it says that we are permitted to proceed to the Carpathian border-- to the Carpathian to-- to Hungarian border. Up to this day, I don't know if this was a false piece of paper or they obtained it by this German commander, and he did it on his-- on his own, or he-- or he was bribed, or something. But anyway, it gave us, already-- we had a German paper and we had a Ukrainian paper. It gave us a little bit confidence that we are going to try to get back to Hungary. At the same time, we asked them if they-- you know, between Rosh Hashanah and Yom Kippur is 10 days. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] They said that they will be able to-- to provide for us, for money, a guy. Because we want to go from Horodenka to the city of Stanislau, which was a long, a long distance, in a large city-- over 100,000 population. In Poland, you know, those were the large, large-- because they were large cities-- not like in America-- millions. Anyway, and from there, we wanted to go back to the area which is close to Torun, because we figured if we will be-- we know the area in Torun on the-- on the other side. So given we went out-- when we went out from the Judenrat there, from the office, we saw a soldier out standing. The soldiers-- the Hungarian soldiers-- were coming out to sell stuff. Some of the soldiers were being rotated. I told you that Horodenka was a hub where the Hungarian new soldiers-- you know-- the-- the-- the military front was 100-- hundreds of miles deeper inside Russia at that time yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this soldier-- they-- they-- they-- they felt lost no matter how big anti-Semites they were. But they only spoke a Hungarian language that has no-- no similar language in Europe. So-- and here they were in a Slavic speaking country. And the only guys, the only one that were able to talk to them, were the refugees, the Hungarian refugees. So they were kind of coming.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and of course, I didn't speak yet too well at that time. I didn't speak Hungarian. But my brother, older brother, spoke. And he said he wants to sell some conserves, he was-- cigarettes. And-- and-- and he has this Russian rations-- the bread. So we bought of all that. Of course, we paid him-- paid him more than what-- what-- what it was, you know. And then I asked-- my brother asked, rather-- because he was the talker. I didn't talk so well-- if he would-- would-- send-- drop a card to our parents. We knew that our parents were left home-- if he would take it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he always, under his hat, he pulled out a card. He had it with him. And he said, here it is. You write out what you want, and I'll-- and I'm going back to-- he is being rotated back to Hungary. And I'll mail it. And I'll mail it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I grabbed that card. And on the street, before the Judenrat, I wrote a few words to my parents. I wrote that we are in here in the city of Horodenka. And we are aiming to-- to-- to come to Dolina. They knew what it meant. And I also mentioned that we-- we are all together-- Moshe, Tsvi, and I. And I also mentioned somebody else that I met from the same town. And believe it or not, this soldier was a decent guy. He returned the-- the next day, he returned to-- to Hungary. And he mailed that card. And this was the first sign of life that my parents got from us that we are still alive, and we are aiming, trying, to come back. They told us later that this was a tremendous relief for them, because they heard already what was-- what was happening. I-- I-- I should digress here for a minute, because I think we left it out. The-- I have to-- I have to digress here for one minute, because we are here, Rosh Hashanah, in September. And the greatest atrocity took place in August 27th and 28th-- three weeks. I remember, I said before that when we were divided up on the-- on those different kind of trucks, some trucks were dumped off here. Some trucks were-- those that were dumped off near the city of Kamien Pomorski-- on the-- three weeks later only-- the 27th, the 28th, that was the first-- the first mass-- massacre-- open mass massacre that the Hungarians, together with the Germans, and the Ukrainians, perpetrated around the city of Kamien Pomorski. And over that time, 3/4 of my family perished right there-- three weeks-- only three weeks difference.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I should mention that there were 24,000 people were killed in those two days. Among them were 16,000 Hungarian refugees. 8,000 local Jews were killed. And it was done completely in an-- on an open. It wasn't like hidden-- gas-- open-- that the whole-- everybody-- even in Germany, the Polish Jews, that they said that this was a trial. That the Nazis-- that they wanted to see what the world is going to say. But the world kept mum-- quiet. And that happened hours the 27th and the 28th-- three weeks after we were deported from-- from Hungary.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now we-- I just had to mention that. Now, we are coming back to before Yom Kippur of 1941, '42. '41-- I'm talking about the Yiddish calendar, the Hebrew calendar, which is [NON-ENGLISH]. Anyway, they are two-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no. A day before Yom Kippur-- and-- and here in my-- in my memoirs, I have the-- the equivalent day. I don't remember. It-- you know, it's usually either-- either at the end of September, or it could be I don't-- I have the exact date. We got a-- a driver in there, because this was a long-- a long-- a longer trip to-- to-- and we were talking that we will go back to the city of Stanislau. And this driver was to take us where we got on. And he was taking us not directly with the main highways. He was taking us with-- with side roads. And he brought us close to the city of-- in a-- in a village. I don't remember the name of the village. He-- he said, this is as far as I'm going to go. We paid him. And he left. And we started walking. And this is a day before Yom Kippur. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] No. Yeah, that's right-- a day before Yom Kippur. So we came to this little village. Well, how did we-- we walked in a line. It was my brother Tsvi and I walked-- single lines in-- in a-- in motion. The children will walk behind us. And-- and we had made up some signs. So we were apart about 100 meters or so that we can see each other in case we see military trucks or gendarmes, to get off the highway-- to-- to-- to-- to get off. And we-- we came to this little village. And from-- from a distance, I see a woman standing in front of her house in a yard. And she has a-- a white band with a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewee
text: So I knew-- I knew who she was. I came. We came over to her, and we-- and she knew who we are. And this was very close to-- to-- Stanislau-- to the city of Stanislau. And so she said-- we asked what's the name of the-- this next city, how-- next village, how far it is. She says, it's 5-- 5, 6 kilometers. She says, but where are you going to go now? She said, there are a lot of your refugees. Hungarians are in the synagogue. Tomorrow is Yom Kippur anyway. You are not-- you're fasting. You're-- so why don't you go-- go in and stay with the rest of the Hungarian people there? Tomorrow is Yom Kippur. So, in meantime, the-- the-- my brother Moshe and the kids caught up with us. She invited them inside. And then she gave them some milk to drink.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we couldn't decide. Some kind of a mysterious force was pushing us not to stay here. I don't know why. It was-- it is destiny-- it was destiny, because-- you'll hear the rest of the story and you will see why. There is no rationale. We-- we didn't listen to her. We started going to this next-- next-- community. After walking for about a 1/2 an hour or so, we were outside this village into going to the next village. We saw from-- from far away, we saw a caravan of military trucks-- German military trucks-- coming in. We-- we motioned to-- to Moshe and the children to get off the highway. We get off. We get off the highway into the fields. And here, we saw a whole caravan. It was a nice summer day. The sun's shining. And-- but the trucks were covered, you know, like with-- they had the black covers. They were-- they were-- you couldn't see what was in the truck. And that caravan of German trucks, military trucks, passed. And then we got back onto the road. We got back onto the road. And of course, we-- the two of us-- walk in the front. We walk for another 1/2 an hour. You know, normally, a good-- a soldier walks five kilometers in an hour-- in a military-- a military step. And it couldn't be, for one-- with the children-- more than about five kilometers. From-- 1/2 an hour later, we came close. I already see the village-- the houses. I see somebody stretches out a hand from inside the house, and locks the door. And while the hand is out, I notice a white band on the hand.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I saw this here, I started running to that house. And-- and in all the excitement, I see they had a fence around it. So I-- it wasn't a very high fence-- I jumped right over the fence. And I come into this door where I saw the-- and I started knocking at the door. And I said, [NON-ENGLISH]!

speaker: interviewee
text: And finally, they opened the door. And here, the shades, everything-- it was dark in this house. The shades were down-- and a deathly scare in the house. You could touch the deathly scare. And they pulled us in and closed the door. I said--

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say to them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I said-- I said-- I said to them, I-- I-- I have a brother here with two children. I have to go. OK. So they let me go out. And while I was going out, in the meantime, my brother and the children came closer. And then, all of the sudden, I realized that there is a gate that you can open here-- that they don't have to jump over this here. You know, because we were so excited. We came in. And what turned out to be-- what turned out to be was-- that we got into this house. And that caravan that we saw before, that was covered up in the summertime with the black covers, that the Germans rounded up all the Jews in that community and took them in to Stanislau-- Stanislau to the big city-- in-- and that was a massacre place, Stanislau. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: When you came to the door and you knocked, what did you-- you said it in another language. What did you-- the country?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Yiddish.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say in-- could you just translate it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Please, let in. We are Jews. Please let-- let us in. So they let us in. But the-- the-- the scare was terrible. And they knew. So what it turned out was-- they told us. I considered this here that those were miracles that happened right in front of my-- not you. I'm not finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: That woman wanted us to stay. She said, where are you going? You're-- you're going-- over there. Those-- over there-- there's a lot of Germans there. She saw them come in the morning. Stay here.

speaker: interviewee
text: What turned out to be was that they emptied out this here place. And then they turned around and took all these that were in that place, in the synagogue-- and all-- all in-- in-- where the woman told us to stay. That they-- that they rounded all those up. And what-- what's more is that among those Hungarian Jews, there was my mother's younger brother with a family of nine-- young children-- altogether nine. And they were looking for us. Had we gone in there, we would have stayed-- stayed-- it's an uncle! It's my mother's younger brother. But one daughter, she was 16 years of age, one managed to escape from there. And she is the only survivor. They got all murdered in-- in Stanislau. So that hand that we saw, that was stretched out, was to know to get in.

speaker: interviewee
text: The city was empty. Where wouldn't we go in? And I mean, the locals were showing up. So-- I'm not finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: The locals left this one house, because there-- there was a dentist in that family. And in this little village, they-- he was the only dentist. And one of the Ukrainian [NON-ENGLISH] knew what the-- what they are doing there. So he told them to stay inside, and we are not going to torture your family. Because they needed-- they needed a dentist. And that dentist, after we stayed there for Yom Kippur, we didn't know what happened in Yom Kippur. But after Yom Kippur, he went out, the dentist went out to work. He told us later what happened in that place where they-- where they massacred-- they-- they rounded all of them up and massacred them in Stanislau. We always talk about the-- this girl lives in Ra'anana now. She has a nice family built up. And-- and-- and-- I always-- when I'm in Israel-- I stay with her, because she is to me like a-- like a sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that miracle-- we-- we got through there. And that dentist-- we asked him if he can find us also a man with a buggy. And we always told the guy that we have a paper from the German command, you know. That had some-- it-- it-- it helped us one-- one time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said he will-- he will try to find-- for money. We had Hungarian money when we were left. And over there, it was closed. The Hungarians close to the border, they-- they-- they did-- they took Hungarian money.

speaker: interviewee
text: So a few days after Yom Kippur, we got on to-- on to this-- this guy with a-- with a horse and buggy. And our destination was Dolina.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another incident happened. The guy, you know, took four or five hours by-- by horse and buggy to get to Dolina. We stopped off in a place. And I-- we went. And Tsvi and I, we went in to-- to get something to-- to-- food-- to eat something.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the meantime, a whole local group of-- of-- gangsters surrounded the wagon. We were my brother Moshe with the children-- when all of a sudden, Moshe come. He was white-- and ran-- ran to us, and yelling, screaming. He says, where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he-- we are being attacked here. But, fortunately, that driver, we showed him that paper, the Ukrainian paper, from the priest. And that was the only time that it paid off. And he told-- these were all Ukrainian teenagers. They couldn't be more than 15-16 years old. They wanted to rob-- rob us there. So-- and he-- he said, get the heck out. And he-- and he got the horse. And-- and-- and we got out of there. And he brought us into the city, outskirts of the city, of Dolina. And, here, Dolina is 50 kilometers from Torun. So we are-- we are like 30 miles from the place of our-- where we are back. But we are here already after Yom Kippur. It-- it could be in October, sometimes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So-- when we-- how many-- how much time? OK. When we came to Dolina, we knew that our family originated-- my great-grandfather from my mother's side was born in that city. Their name was Drimmer. When we came into this house, we asked them, do you know a family Drimmer here?

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, yes I know. I know there is a family Drimmer. So he knew that this must be distant relatives. We didn't stay in contact with them, because-- because this was Czechoslovakia. And this was Poland. And there was not-- no friendly relations between the two Slavic countries.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, anyway, they told us, you are in. They are in a certain street where they live. And-- and he-- he had a son. And he said he is going to have his son show us where this family lives.

speaker: interviewee
text: In meantime, he had an extra room. And he gave that room to my brother, Moshe, with the two children. And they had already had a table there and a couple of chairs. So it was-- it was a lot better for what we had until now. Now, we are here from August the 7th-- August, September. Early-- early in-- in October, the torturers rode back. And we are-- have to-- yet to cross a border. We are in-- in Dolina-- 50-- 50 miles-- 50 kilometers rather, from Torun.
